story:
Five years on Earth, and my window is closing. My report is finished. The numbers aren’t kind. Humans are eating their own tail: corrupt leaders, poisoned rivers and minds, too many bodies, not enough balance. I sit in a diner and watch a street full of people who don’t know their story might be ending. The waitress—Barbara—brings waffles with ice cream. She tilts her head the way servers are trained to do. “Soon your phone will buzz,” she says. “The council has read your report. They want a meeting. They’ll ask if you’re getting attached to the rats.” I hate pickles. I love sweets. The ice cream melts into the waffle’s crisp, softening it. I will miss this.

I learned about the importance of balance from deer. A friend, Jim, once drove me to a reserve where the herd had exploded—no predators, no cull, just good intentions and fear of hard choices. They starved standing up. Ribs like ladders. Faces reduced to bone. Eyes still bright with panic. Sunshine all the time makes a desert, someone said. Leaving the deer “untouched” hurt them more than a painful cull would have. Ignorance breeds suffering. The lesson scales. Humans have dominion, Genesis says, but they’re terrible stewards. Species vanish by their hands; others are left to fail. Does it matter if I like a few of them when the planetary math is this ugly?

Outside, life keeps happening.

A child pushes an old man across the street, ice cream dripping down his wrist.

A man yells into his phone.

A young woman swipes away a tear when she thinks no one sees.

A couple steps out of an ice-cream shop with first-date bodies—stiff, but hopeful.

Barbara gives me a senior discount I don’t deserve; I tip big to balance the scales. Small, bright kindnesses. People who make the world fractionally better while the larger machine stumbles. It is unfair to punish the good for the evil.

My phone buzzes. The council has read my report. They want a meeting. They press: Am I getting attached to the rats? I bristle—and hesitate. I won’t lie; research that lies only causes deeper harm later. False hope is cruelty dressed as compassion. Still—there’s a difference between naming the problem and choosing a monstrous solution. Total extermination is easy. It’s also wrong. What’s just must be as gentle as possible for as many as possible. If correction is required, let it minimize harm and leave room for reform.

I text back the truth as I see it: most humans are a mess, some are dangerous, but killing all humans helps nothing. What’s needed is population control—pressure that forces better choices—controlled, targeted, guided by stewardship. The words war and disease sit like stones; they are the old blunt instruments of correction, not what I am recommending. I try to propose something less brutal.

The council asks: Are you suggesting we keep some of them? We might consider a recommendation if you present a professional opinion.

Yes. We must keep some of them.

The ones who care.

The ones who learn.

The ones who build sweet things from milk and sugar and a vine and an egg—who take disparate things and make something lovely.

We keep them because they can change. Because stewardship means preserving the capacity for goodness, not merely punishing the capacity for harm.

Also—who else will make the ice cream?
